facts.text: Yeehaw Junction got its name from the Yeehaw station on the Florida East Coast Railway's Kissimmee Valley Line that ran through the town from 1915 to 1947.
The Desert Inn, a historic landmark listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1994, served cowboys, lumber workers, and travelers at SR 60/U.S.
In April 2024, a court ordered the Desert Inn property to be sold to V6 Holdings LLC for about $1.1 million after the Osceola County Historical Society owned it.
During late 1968, the U.S. Air Force conducted a Project 112 biological warfare experiment at Yeehaw Junction, testing stem rust agent TX on wheat crops.
Yeehaw Junction sits at the crossroads of US 441, SR 60, and Florida’s Turnpike, roughly 30 miles west of Vero Beach and 30 miles north of Lake Okeechobee.
Some locals say the town’s name sprang from the celebratory “yeehaw!” shout, while others trace it to a Creek word meaning “wolf.”
